evals.prompt: teach me how to pair wines with cheeses. I love hosting dinner parties and I always just guess randomly. I have about 2 months before a big party I'm hosting and I want to actually know what I'm doing by then. I'm not super techy — I mostly just use my laptop for email and browsing.
evals.expected_output: A scaffolded repo with HTML-based lessons, interactive tools (flashcards, quizzes, pairing reference), a dashboard.html progress tracker, and a curriculum.json. Everything should be openable by double-clicking — no terminal commands.
expectations: A dashboard.html file exists at the repo root with visual progress tracking
Lessons are primarily .html files, not .md files (markdown is OK for README and TUTOR_CONTEXT only)
At least two different types of interactive HTML tools exist (e.g., flashcards AND quiz, or reference card AND matching game)
A curriculum.json file is created with modules, lessons, and progress tracking
The curriculum includes a variety of activity types, not just 'read lesson then do exercise'
No technical jargon or terminal commands appear in learner-facing materials

evals.prompt: I'm a junior data scientist (about 1 year of experience with Python and pandas) and I want to get better at machine learning. specifically I want to understand how to build and evaluate classification models. I've got maybe 3-4 months and I can dedicate about an hour a day. teach me
evals.expected_output: A repo with Jupyter notebook lessons (not raw .py files), interactive HTML tools for concept review, a dashboard.html progress tracker, and a curriculum.json. Should include fun/engaging elements alongside the technical content.
expectations: A dashboard.html file exists at the repo root with visual progress tracking
Lessons use Jupyter notebooks (.ipynb) rather than raw .py files for narrative content
At least one interactive HTML tool exists (quiz, flashcards, or cheat sheet)
A curriculum.json file is created with a progression from basic to advanced classification
The curriculum includes variety beyond just notebooks — some interactive review, reference tools, or fun elements
Model evaluation concepts (confusion matrix, precision/recall, cross-validation) are covered

evals.prompt: this is kind of different but... teach me how to be a better listener. I've been getting feedback at work that I interrupt people and jump to solutions too fast, and my partner has said similar things. I don't really know where to start with something like this. it's not like there's a textbook for it.
evals.expected_output: A repo with HTML-based lessons (not flat .md or .txt), interactive self-reflection tools, a dashboard.html progress tracker, and a curriculum.json. Should feel warm and engaging, not like reading a textbook.
expectations: A dashboard.html file exists at the repo root with visual progress tracking
Lessons are primarily .html files that are visually engaging to read
At least one interactive HTML tool for self-reflection or practice (e.g., conversation replay, listening journal, self-check)
A curriculum.json file is created with modules progressing from awareness to practice
Exercises include real-world conversation scenarios, not abstract theory
Tone is warm and non-judgmental throughout — no clinical or lecture-like language
